summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically reviews a new feature in Windows XP Media Center 2005's Update Rollup 2: non-linear stretch (or 'Intelligent Zoom') for displaying 4:3 content on 16:9 widescreen displays. He walks through the limitations of existing solutions—black bars waste screen space, zooming crops content, and uniform stretching distorts the image. The non-linear approach combines slight cropping with progressive edge stretching while keeping the center mostly undistorted. He also warns that persistent black bars can cause plasma TV burn-in, sharing his own experience of visible burn-in after 8 months.
key_points: Non-linear stretch progressively stretches more toward the edges while keeping the center relatively undistorted
Traditional solutions (zoom or uniform stretch) each have significant drawbacks—content loss or distortion
Black bars from 4:3 content can cause permanent burn-in on plasma displays after extended use
Microsoft's 'Intelligent Zoom' represents a practical compromise between competing display approaches
Atwood used white then grey bars to equalize wear on his plasma TV before this feature existed
money_quotes: The image is slightly cropped on the top and bottom, then progressively stretched more towards the edges; the center is barely stretched at all.
After 8 months, our plasma TV had noticeable burn-in on the edges where the "black bars" appear for the 4:3 content!
No content is lost, but we've distorted everything: it's wider and fatter.
Clearly zooming and stretching both have their drawbacks.
key_insight: Non-linear stretch elegantly solves the 4:3-to-16:9 display problem by combining the best aspects of zooming and stretching while minimizing their drawbacks and protecting against screen burn-in.
